rank_note: Rank intentionally null. site/src/data/indexes/us-states.json contains only 21 of 51 jurisdictions; ranks 9-38 were lost in the original legacy-HTML extraction and the surviving entries were renumbered contiguously, so the displayed rank column is not a national rank. Kansas is absent from the published index entirely, so this is a first baseline with no prior composite and no placeholder. Ranks are assigned only when the full 51-jurisdiction index is rebuilt.
calibration_note: Not calibrated to the published us-states rank order, which is known to be corrupt. Kansas is absent from the published index, so this is a first baseline with no placeholder to anchor to. Calibrated to the methodology anchors and to the jurisdictions assessed at full depth under this methodology: Georgia 26.9, Iowa 30.6, Arizona 36.2, Ohio 38.1, North Carolina 45.0, Alaska 47.5, Oregon 50.6, Colorado 52.5, Wisconsin 53.1, Michigan 53.8, Delaware 53.8, Pennsylvania 54.4, Virginia 55.6, Maryland 55.6, New York 56.9, Illinois 60.0, New Jersey 60.6. Kansas at 40.6 sits between Ohio 38.1 and North Carolina 45.0. That placement reflects a state with unusually strong audit and review infrastructure sitting on top of unusually deep unmet need: a nine-year disability waiting list, the nation-leading count of rural hospitals at immediate closure risk, and a fourth consecutive year of foster care settlement failure. Composite verified by running computeCompositeFromDimensions from site/scripts/lib/scoring.mjs (methodology v1.2). Verification output: {"composite":40.6,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}. Inputs AWR 3.0, EMP 2.4, ACT 2.4, EQU 2.6, BND 2.4, ACC 3.0, SYS 3.0, INT 2.2. baseComposite 40.6; stdDev 0.307 so consistencyMult 1.0; 8 of 8 dimensions below 4.0 so weaknessFactor 0 and integrationPremium 0.0; final composite 40.6, band Functional. Because the composite lands within 1.0 of the 40 band boundary, sensitivity cases were run through computeCompositeFromDimensions rather than computed by hand: all dimensions minus 0.2 returns {"composite":35.6,"band":"Developing","integrationPremium":0}; all dimensions plus 0.5 returns {"composite":53.1,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}; INT raised from 2.2 to 2.4 returns {"composite":41.3,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}; EQU raised from 2.6 to 3.2 returns {"composite":42.5,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}. The Functional band is unstable and should be treated as provisional.
attribution_note: Scores Kansas state conduct only, across both political branches, since the benchmark scores the jurisdiction rather than an administration. Kansas was under divided government for the whole 2021-2026 window: a Democratic governor, Laura Kelly, and a Republican Legislature with veto-override supermajorities in both chambers. Many scored items are legislative actions taken over a gubernatorial veto, including the two abortion bills overridden on 2026-04-09 and the twenty bills enacted over veto during the 2026 session. These are scored as state conduct, and the veto is recorded as mitigating evidence within the same subdimension rather than as a separate entity. The reverse also applies: executive-branch achievements such as the Community Supports Waiver approval and the maternal health grant are scored as state conduct even where the Legislature was not the actor. Federal policy imposed on Kansas is excluded. The 2025 federal reconciliation law changed Medicaid financing nationally and the resulting projected 4 billion dollar funding loss is not scored against the state. What is scored is the state discretion Kansas has exercised and continues to exercise, namely its decision not to expand Medicaid, which is a choice made by the Kansas Legislature and is the direct cause of the roughly 28,000-person coverage gap. Similarly, the 2025 federal suspension of refugee admissions and the federal refugee benefit changes are federal actions and are not scored; the Kansas Office for Refugees is scored only as evidence of a state-designated coordination structure. The 2012-2017 tax experiment and its reversal fall largely outside the 2021-2026 window. It is scored only under AB4 Systemic Learning and AB2 Correction Willingness, where the institutional learning is the relevant and durable fact, and is not used to inflate any current-service dimension.

cross_state_consistency_flag.reasoning: Limb (a) is not satisfied. The Kansas Legislature has not exempted itself from the Kansas Open Records Act; KORA has applied to the Legislature since the 1980s and still does. Nor has Kansas curtailed its oversight body: the Legislative Division of Post Audit retains its remit, its independence and its funding, and issued 18 performance audits in fiscal year 2025. What Kansas shows instead is enforcement obstruction rather than structural self-exemption. The state paid 20,000 dollars for outside counsel to defend a senator against a KORA complaint brought by a newspaper, and the Attorney General has a standing conflict between representing state officials and enforcing KORA. Under the North Dakota refinement, procedural and enforcement weakness is scored on the merits and is not collapsed into the cap. Under the Idaho refinement, KORA subject-matter carve-outs alone do not trigger the rule either. AB3 is therefore scored 3 on the merits: Post Audit and the Office of the Child Advocate publish damaging, uncompelled findings, but a citizen cannot reliably compel production from the legislative branch.
cross_state_consistency_flag.scoring_effect: No effect on the composite as scored. AB3 is scored 3 on the merits, which is the same value the cap would have produced, so the composite is 40.6 either way. Had the rule instead been applied as a floor of 2, ACC would fall from 3.0 to 2.8 and the composite would be 40, band Developing.

band_stability_flag.note: The composite of 40.6 sits 0.6 points above the boundary between the Developing band (21-40) and the Functional band (41-60). Sensitivity cases run through computeCompositeFromDimensions: all dimensions minus 0.2 returns 35.6 Developing; all dimensions plus 0.5 returns 53.1 Functional; INT raised to 2.4 returns 41.3 Functional; EQU raised to 3.2 returns 42.5 Functional. The Functional band should be treated as provisional.

key_evidence: Kansas is one of only ten states that has not expanded Medicaid. About 28,000 low-income Kansans sit in the coverage gap, earning too little for subsidised private cover and too much to qualify for Medicaid.
Kansas has more rural hospitals at immediate risk of closing than any other state. Of 100 open inpatient hospitals, 69 are at risk of closing and 28 are at immediate risk, reported 2026-06-11.

Kansans with intellectual or developmental disabilities wait about nine years for waiver services. The I/DD waiver served 9,399 people with 4,320 waiting as of 2025-02-05. The new Community Supports Waiver adds 500 slots in its first year.
Kansas failed its foster care settlement for a fourth straight year. Children entering care in 2024 were moved 7.24 times per 1,000 days against an agreed target of 4.4.

Kansas voters rejected the Value Them Both amendment by nearly 20 points on 2022-08-02. The Legislature passed abortion restrictions in 2023, overrode vetoes in 2024 and again on 2026-04-09, and has put abortion rights back on the ballot for 2026-08-04.

The 2012 tax cuts were reversed by a bipartisan veto override on 2017-06-07 after revenues fell and school and road spending was cut. This is a rare documented case of a state reversing its own flagship policy.

The Legislative Division of Post Audit issued 18 performance audits in fiscal year 2025, including a finding that 7 of 15 audited state entities did not substantially comply with IT security standards.
Kansas allocated 20,000 dollars for outside counsel to defend a state senator against an open records complaint brought by a newspaper under the state own records law.
